core_claim: Microsoft suspended Nightmare Eclipse's GitHub account after they released six Windows zero-days (BlueHammer, RedSun, UnDefend, YellowKey, GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma). GitHub terminated the account on May 23, 2026; GitLab on May 26. The researcher resurfaced as MSNightmare and dropped two more: RoguePlanet (7th, June 9-10) and GreatXML (8th, June 11). Microsoft threatened legal action via its Digital Crimes Unit around May 28, then walked it back after community backlash. The disclosure standoff highlights broken vendor-researcher dynamics.

caption:
Microsoft suspended Nightmare Eclipse's GitHub account after they released six Windows zero-days. The researcher resurfaced as MSNightmare and kept going.

The original six (pre-ban, April through May 2026): BlueHammer (Defender update-flow race to SYSTEM), RedSun (Defender write redirected to System32), UnDefend (disables Defender silently), YellowKey (BitLocker bypass via USB), GreenPlasma (SYSTEM via CTFMON), MiniPlasma (incomplete-fix regression). All patched as of June 2026 Patch Tuesday. Multiple exploited in the wild before patches shipped.

GitHub terminated the account on May 23, 2026 and wiped six repos. GitLab suspended the mirror on May 26. Microsoft's Digital Crimes Unit threatened criminal prosecution around May 28. Community backlash forced a clarification that it would not pursue researchers. The account stayed suspended.

The researcher resurfaced as MSNightmare. RoguePlanet (7th, June 9-10) and GreatXML (8th, June 11) followed the ban, both confirmed working on fully patched systems.

You cannot rely on coordinated disclosure to protect you. When the process breaks, exploits go public immediately.

Build detection for the behaviors, not just the CVEs: Defender tampering, stalled signature updates, System32 writes, BitLocker bypass attempts. The researcher's frustration became your adversary's toolkit.

Do you have detection for Defender tampering, stalled updates, and System32 writes?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[Nightmare Eclipse, Microsoft, GitHub, responsible disclosure, zero-day, BlueHammer, RedSun, UnDefend, YellowKey, GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma, RoguePlanet, detection engineering]
comment_prompt: Do you have detection for Defender tampering, stalled updates, and System32 writes?
alt_text: Title card: Microsoft suspended the researcher, and the exploits reappeared. The Nightmare Eclipse account was removed, the zero-days republished under MSNightmare with no warning. A single dark silhouetted figure half dissolves into drifting purple static, its left half solid and whole.

Nightmare Eclipse dropped six Windows zero-days before the ban: BlueHammer, RedSun, UnDefend, YellowKey, GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma. GitHub terminated the account May 23. Two more followed post-ban: RoguePlanet and GreatXML. A dark ledger of six entries.

GitHub suspended the account without warning, and the exploits reappeared on MSNightmare within hours, with RoguePlanet validated on fully patched Windows 10 and 11. Microsoft said it would not pursue researchers, yet the suspension stood. A broken chain.

The original six: BlueHammer abused Defender's update flow to SYSTEM, RedSun redirected Defender's write into System32, UnDefend disables Defender, YellowKey bypasses BitLocker via USB, GreenPlasma gets full SYSTEM via CTFMON, MiniPlasma exploits an incomplete 2020 fix. Six distinct keys, all now patched.

Where the process broke: the researcher disclosed privately first, claims Microsoft ignored or mishandled the reports, then went public after frustration, and Microsoft replied with legal threats and suspension. A cracked negotiation table with broken comm lines.

The defender lesson: you cannot rely on coordinated disclosure, because when it breaks exploits go public immediately. Monitor Defender tampering, stalled updates, System32 writes, and BitLocker bypass attempts. A vendor shield cracks beside a detection radar.

Coordinated disclosure is a courtesy, not a guarantee. When vendors break trust, researchers go public, so defenders must detect the behaviors regardless. A fractured handshake, one hand purple for vendor and one red for researcher, leaks light.
Closing question: Do you have detection for Defender tampering, stalled updates, and System32 writes? A single small glowing purple emblem sits on a clean dark end-card with open negative space.

sources.supports: Confirms GreatXML as 8th exploit (June 11). Explicitly names the pre-ban six as RedSun, UnDefend, BlueHammer, YellowKey, GreenPlasma, and MiniPlasma (not RoguePlanet): 'The earlier six - RedSun, UnDefend, BlueHammer, YellowKey, GreenPlasma, and MiniPlasma - all have patches as of this week's Patch Tuesday event.'

sources.source: Cyderes Howler Cell: RoguePlanet - Windows Zero-Day That Weaponizes Defender's Own Quarantine Pipeline
sources.supports: GitHub terminated Nightmare-Eclipse account May 23 wiping all six repos; GitLab suspended May 26; RoguePlanet is the 7th exploit released June 10 under MSNightmare handle; patch status for all six pre-ban exploits (BlueHammer CVE-2026-33825, RedSun CVE-2026-41091, UnDefend CVE-2026-45498, YellowKey CVE-2026-45585, GreenPlasma CVE-2026-45586, MiniPlasma patched June 9 Patch Tuesday); RoguePlanet confirmed on fully patched Windows 11.

narration.text: A security researcher called Nightmare Eclipse released six Windows zero-days in the space of a few months, and GitHub suspended the account on May 23 without warning, wiping all six repositories.

The exploits covered different layers of Windows: one abused Defender's signature-update process to gain SYSTEM, another redirected Defender's own write into System32, a third silently disabled Defender, and one bypassed BitLocker over USB.
The researcher says private disclosure came first, but that Microsoft ignored or mishandled the reports, so they eventually went public out of frustration.

After the suspension, the researcher resurfaced as MSNightmare and dropped two more exploits: RoguePlanet, the seventh, confirmed working on fully patched Windows 10 and 11, and GreatXML, an eighth that bypassed BitLocker.

Microsoft initially threatened legal action, which triggered heavy community backlash, then walked back the threat and said it would not pursue researchers, but the account remained suspended and the exploits remained public.
This is the real lesson for defenders: coordinated disclosure is not a protection you can rely on, so you need detection that catches the underlying behaviors regardless of whether a patch exists.
That means alerting on Defender tampering or disablement, stalled signature updates, unexpected writes into System32, and BitLocker bypass attempts from removable media.
Do you already have coverage for these behaviors in your environment? Drop your answer in the comments.

beats.caption: Six zero-days. Multiple exploited in the wild.
Private disclosure ignored. Legal threats followed.
GitHub suspended. MSNightmare up. Patched systems hit.
Backlash forced clarification. Account still suspended.
Detect behaviors not CVEs. SAM, System32, Defender tampering.
Disclosure is courtesy not guarantee. Detect anyway.

video.subtitle_style: large centered lower-third, high contrast, two lines max
